name: Seventy Seven Alley
chef: Chef London Chase (formerly of Essential by Christophe and Mango Bay) commands a sleek hotel dining room. Precisely plated seafood delicacies served at the tasting counter or à la carte.

name: Kjun
chef: Chef Jenny Kwak, a pioneer of Korean-Cajun fusion, returns with a bold bi-level space. Named one of The Infatuation's Best New Restaurants of 2022, the new space adds a full bar and formal tasting menu upstairs.

name: Kidilum
chef: Chef Vinu Raveendran, formerly of Dubai's Carnival by Trèsind, leads the kitchen. Celebrating Kerala's coastal cuisine with spice-rich seafood, meats, and seasonal produce.

name: Kees
chef: From the legendary PDT (Please Don't Tell) team, creators of New York's most celebrated speakeasy. Kees applies the same obsessive craft to classic cocktail formats — martinis, Negronis, and sours.

name: Ambassadors Clubhouse
chef: From the team behind London Mayfair's Gymkhana and Brigadiers. Executive Chef Dhruv Mittal's butter chicken chops, prawn curry, and a bi-level space draped in regal decor.

name: Cleo Downtown
chef: Halley Chambers and Kip Gleize of Three Top Hospitality open their first Manhattan restaurant. Golden rotisserie chickens, herb sauces, market salads with natural wine and optional caviar.

name: Odo East Village
chef: Chef Hiroki Odo, whose Flatiron flagship earned Michelin recognition, opens this more accessible 24-seat counter sibling. An izakaya-meets-kaiseki experience with à la carte seasonal bites.

name: Beto's Carnitas
chef: A beloved NYC pop-up turned permanent fast-casual. Beto's earned cult status for its slow-cooked carnitas and rotating guisados. The Clinton Street brick-and-mortar is founder Beto Gutierrez's first permanent home.

name: Umeko
chef: The team behind Ume expands to Manhattan with this West Village counter. A $48 prix-fixe don menu of seasonal chirashi and tuna sets alongside overflowing maki rolls.

name: Aperitivo by CARTA
chef: The all-day café and wine bar concept from the CARTA team brings Italian aperitivo culture to the West Village. Natural wine program, spritz service, and light bites from morning through late night.

name: Pizza Studio Tamaki
chef: Chef Tamaki, a Japanese-trained Neapolitan pizzaiolo, brings a perfectionist approach to St. Marks Place. Pre-opening pop-ups generated significant buzz for the classic margherita and egg-and-sausage pie.

name: Seirēn
chef: A Portugal-and-Spain-inspired conservas and cocktail bar. Notable for inventive drinks using MSG and parmesan tequila, plus preserved seafood on sourdough. The space doubles as a tattoo studio during daytime.

name: Much Obliged
chef: From the team behind Greenpoint's beloved Gator bar. Much Obliged brings their low-key neighborhood spirit to Alphabet City — Corsican wines, fried fish sandwiches, and inventive house cocktails.

name: Golden Steer
chef: Helmed by the team behind the legendary Las Vegas original open since 1958. The NYC outpost preserves tableside service traditions and Rat Pack-era grandeur at the storied 1 Fifth Ave address.

name: Tacos La 36
chef: A taqueria bringing six rotating taco varieties to Midtown including vegetarian options, ceviche, beer, and cocktails. A happy hour program makes it a rare casual value destination.

name: Rulin
chef: The husband-and-wife team behind Brooklyn's Noodle Lane bring pan-Chinese noodle expertise to Manhattan. The intimate Union Square room spotlights hand-pulled Lanzhou beef noodle soup and dan dan noodles.

name: Bistrot Ha
chef: The team behind LES phenomenon Ha's Snack Bar expands with this larger sibling. The kitchen blends French technique with Vietnamese soul — fried yuba with crab, curried lobster, and sweetbreads vol au vent.

name: Babbo
chef: Stephen Starr acquired the iconic Mario Batali restaurant and brought in Mark Ladner (formerly of Del Posto) to revitalize the kitchen. Beloved Italian classics alongside Del Posto's legendary 100-layer lasagna.

name: Wild Cherry
chef: From James Beard Award–winning chefs Lee Hanson and Riad Nasr (Frenchette, Le Rock, Le Veau d'Or), in partnership with indie film studio A24 inside the restored Cherry Lane Theatre. A 45-seat supper club with a horseshoe bar, green banquettes, and a menu running from oysters and tuna crudo to kielbasa with kraut, a standout cheeseburger, and steak for two. One of the buzziest downtown openings of fall 2025.

name: Meadow Lane
chef: A wellness-forward counter-service spot serving artisanal chicken nuggets, cauliflower dishes, and market-driven bowls. The West Village location draws health-conscious diners seeking clean ingredients.

name: Salumeria Rosi East Village
chef: The beloved Upper West Side Italian restaurant and grocer expands to the East Village, with breakfast, lunch, and dinner plus panini stuffed with imported mortadella and bresaola all day.

name: Kaia Wine Bar
chef: One of New York's only South African restaurants relocated 10 blocks south on the Upper East Side. Over 50 South African wines by the glass complement peri-peri deviled eggs, lamb burgers, and a globally-inspired menu.

name: Double Knot
chef: The Philadelphia import brings its chef's tasting and izakaya-style format to Midtown — 10 courses of small bites and sushi plus soft serve for under $100. The à la carte menu shines with handrolls and robatayaki.

name: Seahorse
chef: From restaurateur John McDonald (Lure Fishbar, Bowery Meat Company) and Executive Chef John Villa. Located in the W Hotel Union Square lobby, the meticulously designed room features a blue-tiled raw bar, a sweeping 40-foot Rhapsody in Blue mural, and a seafood-forward menu of crudos, grilled prawns, tuna au poivre, and lobster pasta.

name: Hwaro
chef: Chef Sungchul Shim (Michelin-starred Kochi, Mari) opens this 22-seat circular chef's counter hidden inside Gui Steakhouse. Named after the traditional Korean 화로 charcoal brazier, Hwaro serves a 13-course tasting menu at $295, blending Korean heritage with New York's creative pulse. One of the city's most intimate and transportive dining experiences.

name: Or'esh
chef: Michelin-starred Chef Nadav Greenberg (formerly of Shmoné) leads the kitchen at this Catch Hospitality Group (Corner Store, Eighty Six) production. A live-fire Mediterranean concept centered on a custom charcoal grill — handcrafted Jerusalem bagels, dry-aged hamachi, wagyu strip, and spicy spinach gomiti, all kissed by coal. Already one of the hardest reservations in NYC.

name: The Eighty Six
chef: From Catch Hospitality Group (Corner Store, Catch), co-owners Eugene Remm and Tilman Fertitta, with Chef Michael Vignola. Set in the former Chumley's speakeasy at 86 Bedford Street — a Prohibition-era literary haunt where Hemingway once drank. Only 35 seats, Art Deco interiors by Rockwell Group, and one of the hardest reservations in the city.

name: Adda
chef: Chef Chintan Pandya and restaurateur Roni Mazumdar (Dhamaka, Semma) reopen their groundbreaking Queens Indian canteen in a larger East Village home. Named Time Out NYC's #1 restaurant of 2025. The tableside Butter Chicken Experience — prepared by the chef — is the main event, alongside chaatwala servers roaming the room and cult classics like bheja masala, goat biryani, and Nagaland pork fry.

name: Kabawa
chef: Chef Paul Carmichael and Momofuku transform the former Ko space in East Village's Extra Place alley into a celebration of Caribbean cuisine. Named Eater NYC's Best New Restaurant of 2025. A $145 three-course prix fixe rooted in Carmichael's Barbadian childhood — pepper shrimp with sorrel, braised goat, breadfruit toston, and coconut cream cheese turnovers. The adjacent Bar Kabawa pours daiquiris and West Indies patties.

name: Cove
chef: Chef Flynn McGarry (Gem, Eleven Madison Park, Alinea) opens his most personal restaurant in Hudson Square. A 90-seat room McGarry designed himself — Douglas fir floors, custom ceramics, West Coast modernism — offering an eight-course kitchen counter tasting and an à la carte main dining room. Menus change with the seasons: lobster with chanterelles, grilled eggplant with truffle, tomato consommé. Wine Director Paris McGarry curates 400 low-intervention bottles.

name: Saverne
chef: Two-Michelin-starred Chef Gabriel Kreuther — who held two stars at his eponymous Midtown restaurant for years, and before that at The Modern inside MoMA — opens this wood-fired brasserie as a more elemental sibling concept. Named for the Alsatian town where he grew up. Open kitchen burning applewood, oak, and cherry. Seafood, meats, and vegetables cooked over fire.

name: Da Toscano
chef: The Greenwich Village Italian fixture — beloved for its octopus carpaccio and lamb neck agnolotti — relocated from Minetta Lane to the Iroquois Hotel in Midtown. Chef Riccardo Orfino adds all-day breakfast and lunch service alongside the full Italian dinner menu plus a full Italian coffee bar.

name: Shin Takumi
chef: The most approachable sibling in the Takumi family — which includes Sushi YOLO and Takumi Omakase — opens in the West Village. The $58 12-course format (10 nigiri, one handroll, dessert) varies daily based on available fish, offering serious omakase craft at a fraction of the usual price point.

name: Chubby Tan
chef: The first US outpost of Tokyo-founded Gyutan no Lemon, bringing Sendai-style gyutan-yaki (grilled beef tongue) to the East Village. Sendai is Japan's undisputed capital of beef tongue — a regional obsession dating back to post-WWII, elevated into a civic institution. The signature Beef Tongue Rice is the main event.

name: Bar Lola
chef: Named for Irish actress and dancer Lola Montez, this 125-seat two-floor café and cocktail bar on Restaurant Row channels Spanish wine bars and French bistros through a New York lens. Crème Brûlée Espresso Martinis, Breakfast Martinis, Hot Amaro Lattes, natural wine, and a strong coffee program.

name: Birdie's
chef: A solo venture from industry veteran Arianna Tettamanzi. Six rotating flavors of frozen yogurt with unconventional toppings — olive oil, Cinnamon Toast Crunch, seasonal fruit — in a tiny 7th Avenue South storefront. The Infatuation called it the anchor of NYC's 2026 fro-yo renaissance.

name: Oriana
chef: From Chef Andy Quinn and Sommelier Cedric Nicaise — the Eleven Madison Park veterans who opened The Noortwyck in 2022. A wood-fired American restaurant with a 7,000-bottle old-world wine cellar. Oysters, caviar service, grilled fish, coal-roasted chicken, beef ribs, dry-aged duck.

name: Jeju Noodle Bar Nolita
chef: Chef Douglas Kim — who trained at Bouley and Per Se before opening the original Jeju Noodle Bar in the West Village in 2017, earning it the first-ever Michelin star awarded to a noodle restaurant in the US, a distinction held for seven consecutive years — opens this Nolita second location with new exclusive dishes.

name: Dean's
chef: From the team behind King — the beloved SoHo Italian from chefs Annie Shi and Clare de Boer — comes this British seafood companion opening next door. Fish pie, roasted Scottish langoustines, potted shrimp on hot buttered crumpets, and Guinness on draft.

name: Good Time Country Buffet
chef: From the team behind Kisa — the acclaimed Korean prix-fixe on the Lower East Side — comes this wildly unexpected genre switch: a Southern country buffet inspired by the Korean-American founders' childhood in Atlanta. Fried chicken, cornbread, collard greens, served buffet-style in the former Moody Tongue Pizza space.

name: Straker's NYC
chef: Chef-owner Thomas Straker brings his British-by-way-of-Italy concept into Keith McNally's former Lucky Strike space in SoHo. Seasonal flatbreads, seabass tartare, and wood-fired meats.

name: Dishoom
chef: The beloved London institution finally arrives in New York. Dishoom's Bombay café concept — iconic black daal, breakfast naan rolls, and chai — has sold out every UK location since 2010.

name: Uovo
chef: The LA pasta phenomenon built on fresh pasta overnighted daily from Bologna — a 1950s bolognese recipe, each order made individually. Growing from one to five LA locations in rapid succession.

name: Oyatte
chef: Chef Hasung Lee's debut fine-dining venture — pedigree spanning The French Laundry, Atomix, Gramercy Tavern, and Geranium Copenhagen. A seasonal tasting menu guided by restraint and clarity.

name: Carver 48
chef: From Carver Road Hospitality CEO Sean Christie and Executive Chef Daniel Ontiveros (Joël Robuchon at The Mansion, MGM Grand). Spinoff of award-winning Carversteak Las Vegas.

name: RYE by Martin Auer
chef: Austria's celebrated Auer baking family brings their generational craft to SoHo. The concept centers on a 100% rye sourdough loaf — hand-shaped, naturally leavened, 24-hour fermented.

name: Bufón
chef: The swanky Lower East Side follow-up to West Village wine bar Demo, from chefs Quang Nguyen and Dina Fan. Bluefin tuna carpaccio, beans with dried scallops, grilled mackerel, steak selection, and fries. Robust natural wine list curated by Jacob Nass and Pierre Derrien.

name: Giulietta
chef: An 11,000-square-foot all-day Italian from La Pecora Bianca owner Mark Barak at the base of the MetLife Building. Culinary director Ed Scarpone (Daniel Boulud, Stephen Starr) runs a menu of whipped ricotta toast, Neapolitan pizzas, pastas, and two-pound lobster fra diavolo. Mid-century coastal Italian design with 275 seats.

name: Verde
chef: From Yeels Group (Paris, Saint Tropez, Dubai), Verde features large-format meats, a wide selection of seafood and crudos, pastas, and pizzas. The draw is the nightly transformation: when dinner fades, a DJ and party take over. First US location.

name: Anbā
chef: A 10-seat omakase counter led by an all-women culinary team. Chef Ambrely Ouimette trained under Nobu Matsuhisa and offers a 16-course tasting menu ($220) rooted in classical Japanese techniques, fermentation, and aging. Front cocktail lounge transitions to late-night DJ program.

name: Dahla
chef: Modern Thai with dishes like khao soi featuring homemade mafaldine and potato terrine topped with caviar and Thai basil aioli. Creative cocktails include the Marigold (soba cha, shiitake, parmesan crisp) and the Osaka Fern (strawberry gin, matcha, Calpico).

name: Delos
chef: Part of a boom of new Greek openings across NYC. Family-style menu leaning heavily on seafood — raw and cooked preparations — plus traditional dishes like spanakopita and dolmadakia. A $35 two-course prix fixe lunch is available daily.

name: Lily Pond
chef: A dreamy 25-seat bistro with daily brunch service. Club sandwich, avocado burger, French toast, and a kimchi Reuben. Korean influences via coloring pages and tea service. LA's Bravo Toast coffee program. The aesthetic channels summer in the East Hamptons and Monet's water lilies.

name: Nounou
chef: A noodle bar serving brothy and dry bowls drawing on global flavors. Potato noodles in Thai chile-lime broth with seafood, squid ink pasta with crabmeat and brown butter, udon with steak and Sichuan peppercorn, spinach noodles and chicken in miso broth.

name: Piadi by La Piadineria
chef: Italy's biggest fast-casual chain (500+ locations) makes its American debut at Madison Square Park. Specializes in piadina — thin Emilia-Romagna flatbread freshly rolled, baked, and stuffed with prosciutto, soft cheese, and arugula. $11–$17 per piadina.

name: Bar Maeda
chef: From bartender Yoshikatsu Maeda, an alum of the World's 50 Best-recognized Mori Bar in Tokyo. Martinis made with Japanese gin. Attached to omakase restaurant Sushidokoro Mekumi.

name: Cô Lạc
chef: Helen Nguyen, owner of Saigon Social, opened this Vietnamese seafood restaurant in the former Van Đa space. A fresh addition to the East Village's ever-evolving Vietnamese dining scene.

name: Skëwr
chef: Inside the Park South Hotel, Skëwr focuses on shareable wood-fired cooking. The menu features a range of meat and vegetable skewers plus large-format dishes like whole branzino and meze.

name: Sushi Yukimi
chef: An intimate omakase room named for the Japanese concept of 'viewing snow.' A 17-course menu ($189/person) led by a sushi sous chef from Michelin-starred Sushi Nakazawa. Currently BYOB.

name: Lumo Ombro
chef: Café by day, cocktail bar by night. Chef Steven Hubbell (St. Anselm, Michelin-starred Junoon) serves cinnamon-braised beef cheek, glazed mushrooms on grilled masa. Bartender Christopher Lockwood makes olive oil martinis. Curated loose-leaf teas and a warming Champurrado.

name: Salt Bread Ko
chef: K-town's buzzy new Korean salt bread bakery. Buttery, airy salt bread baked by the hour — from savory (caviar-and-scallion cream cheese) to sweet (raspberry-lychee jam). Flavor dips include kimchi bacon, strawberry, and pandan coconut.

name: Hi Dozo (Nakazawa)
chef: DoorDash-exclusive sushi concept from Daisuke Nakazawa — the legendary sushi master behind Michelin-starred Sushi Nakazawa. Opening near Rockefeller Center with delivery and takeout, bringing his exacting Edomae technique to a new format.

name: Lou & Bev's
chef: An all-day bakery and pizza restaurant with a wine bar from the Confidant team (Henry Goldman, Martin Borkan). Opening next door to the recently relocated Confidant in Brooklyn Heights.

name: Giardino at Giulietta
chef: The outdoor aperitivo bar and garden extension of Giulietta at the MetLife Building. 150 seats serving spritzes, frozen drinks, and pizzas. Converts to a heated chalet-style lounge in winter.

name: Nonna Dora's Tribeca
chef: About 20 kinds of pasta per day are lovingly handmade by the real Nonna Dora — octogenarian Addolorata Marzovilla. A women-operated Tribeca trattoria where every dish feels like it was made by your Italian grandmother. One of the most heartwarming dining experiences in the city.

name: Hakata Tonton
chef: You truly can't keep one of the city's favorite spots for pork-centric hot pot down. Now in its third iteration, Hakata Tonton has been beloved for its unique and supremely comforting homestyle Japanese cooking. A cult comeback in Chinatown.

name: Beast & Butterflies Downtown
chef: An elegant restaurant and bar inside the FiDi M Social Hotel. Spanish-inspired tapas, cocktails, and lovely views of the World Trade Center next door. The downtown outpost of a hotel restaurant group.

name: Lei
chef: From one of the owners of King and Jupiter — two of SoHo's most beloved restaurants. Lei brings a fresh take on the wine bar, pairing an eclectic, globally sourced selection of wines with traditional Chinese flavors. Critically acclaimed and women-operated.

name: Quick Eternity
chef: Step inside Melville's Moby Dick at this bar inspired by the novel's whaling tavern. Stiff cocktails, a satisfying Manhattan chowder, and an indulgent Jamaican rum caramel sticky toffee pudding. All reservations are for 'The Gam' — a communal experience.

name: Teakwood Thai Chophouse
chef: Thai classics meet chophouse standards with a twist. Pad Thai and kaprow alongside pork chops roasted with cashews and pineapple tomato chutney. Baguette garlic bread with cilantro adds a playful edge. A unique fusion concept in Tribeca.

name: BINONDO
chef: Named for the Chinatown district of Manila, Binondo serves Filipino Chinese classics in a warm and inviting space. Don't skip the pork sisig or okoy, a shrimp fritter specialty. One of the few Filipino restaurants in lower Manhattan.

name: Farzi NYC
chef: The first New York outpost of a global restaurant group known for its innovative takes on Indian cuisine. NYC-exclusive dishes include goat cheese chicken tikka and keema pie. Modern Indian in a stylish Tribeca setting.

name: Wok In Duane
chef: Women-operated spot serving shumai, black pepper beef, and a standout scallop-shrimp-chicken sausage fried rice. Cozy, warm-lit space perfect for a casual night out. The kind of neighborhood gem you want to keep to yourself.

name: The Foyer at NY Vintners
chef: The next best thing to being a member of the exclusive NY Vintners club — but open to the public. An ideal place to enjoy a glass of whatever your server recommends from their vast and excellent cellar. Brand new on Resy in Tribeca.

name: NORIMEN
chef: A brand new Korean spot in Manhattan, freshly listed on Resy with a perfect 5.0 rating. Details still emerging — an early discovery for those who like being first.

name: Conwell Hall
chef: A swanky cocktail bar tucked inside a former bank built in the 1920s. Art Deco interiors create the perfect backdrop for a Sazerac and an order of crab artichoke dip. One of FiDi's most atmospheric new arrivals.
